passage:
Text 1
A school district introduced a reading program that required students to read independently for 20 minutes each school day. After one year, district administrators reported that students who participated in the program showed improved reading comprehension scores. Teachers also noted that students who read most consistently tended to perform better on vocabulary assessments than those who read less frequently.

Note from the district's assessment office: End-of-year reading comprehension test results showed the following average score improvements from the prior year:

| Reading Frequency (days per week) | Average Score Improvement |
|---|---|
| 1–2 days | +4 points |
| 3–4 days | +9 points |
| 5 days (every day) | +14 points |

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from a school district literacy program report

stem: Based on the text and the note, what do the data suggest about reading frequency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Students who read more days per week showed greater score improvements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Students who read every day showed the same improvement as those reading 3-4 days.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reading frequency had no effect on comprehension score improvements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Students who read 1-2 days per week improved their scores the most.

explanation: The text states that students who read most consistently performed better on assessments. The note's data confirm this pattern numerically: students reading 1-2 days per week improved by 4 points, those reading 3-4 days improved by 9 points, and those reading every day (5 days) improved by 14 points. Each step up in reading frequency corresponds to a larger score gain, directly supporting choice A.

distractorRationale.B: The note shows that students reading every day improved by 14 points while those reading 3-4 days improved by only 9 points — a difference of 5 points. This distractor directly contradicts the data.
distractorRationale.C: Both the text and the note show clear differences in outcomes based on reading frequency, making this claim contradicted by every piece of evidence in the passage.
distractorRationale.D: The data show that students reading 1-2 days per week had the smallest improvement (+4 points), not the largest. This distractor reverses the actual direction of the trend.
